did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses an ensemble of deep learning models (VGGNet, CapsNet, and ResNet) for skin cancer detection using the ISIC public dataset. The study employs convolutional neural networks to automatically extract features and classify skin lesions as benign or malignant, evaluating performance via sensitivity, accuracy, specificity, F-score, and precision.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of an ensemble deep learning model (combining VGGNet, CapsNet, and ResNet) for skin cancer detection. It describes a specific methodology, utilizes the ISIC public dataset, and reports new experimental results including accuracy (93.5%), sensitivity, specificity, F-score, and precision, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Skin Cancer Detection Using Combined Decision of Deep Learners
brief_summary: This study addresses skin cancer detection by developing an ensemble deep learning model that combines the decisions of VGGNet, CapsNet, and ResNet. The researchers utilized the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) public dataset, specifically selecting 3000 malignant and 2800 benign dermoscopic images for binary classification. The methodology involved training individual convolutional neural networks and merging their outputs using a majority weighting scheme to enhance prediction accuracy. Performance was evaluated using sensitivity, accuracy, specificity, F-score, and precision metrics. The results demonstrated that the combined decision of the deep learners was superior to the findings of individual learners across all evaluated metrics. Consequently, the study concludes that this ensemble approach offers a compelling method for improving skin cancer detection accuracy.

lesion_types_studied.details: Used as one of the two binary classes in the experiment. The dataset included 3000 malignant images (balanced with benign images). Melanoma is mentioned as the most threatening skin cancer type in the introduction.
Used as the other binary class in the experiment. The dataset included 2800 benign images. The study performed binary classification between benign and malignant classes.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into malignant or benign categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification using the ISIC dataset with 3000 malignant and 2800 benign images. The model combines decisions from VGGNet, CapsNet, and ResNet using a majority weighting scheme to predict discrete diagnostic labels (benign vs. malignant). Performance is evaluated using sensitivity, accuracy, specificity, F-score, and precision.

datasets_used.name: ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collaboration) Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the ISIC images repository containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions. The study utilized a subset of this dataset for binary classification of skin cancer.

use.training: Used to train the individual deep learning models (VGGNet, CapsNet, and ResNet). 80% of the selected images (4640 total) were used for training.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the performance of the ensemble model and individual learners. 20% of the selected images (1160 total) were used as test data.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between the proposed ensemble model and individual learners were based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, F-score, precision) without statistical significance testing.

ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collabo.notes: The proposed ensemble model outperformed individual learners (VGGNet, CapsNet, ResNet) and other machine learning approaches (SVM, KNN, Naive Bayes, Random Forest) in terms of accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, F-score, and precision. The ensemble achieved the best accuracy of 93.5% and the best loss function of 0.06. Hyperparameters for the proposed model included 40 epochs, a learning rate of 0.001, a batch size of 32, and a stochastic gradient descent (SGD) optimizer.

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble of VGG16, CapsNet, and ResNet

VGG16.structure: Five blocks of convolutional layers followed by pooling, ending with three fully connected layers. Block 1: 64 filters. Block 2: 128 filters. Block 3: 256 filters. Block 4: 512 filters. Block 5: 512 filters.

CapsNet.squashing_function: Used to shorten vectors to length slightly less than 1

ResNet.final_layers: Average pooling and softmax for classification

training_details.loss_function: Not explicitly named for the ensemble, but individual training losses reported: VGG16 (0.40), CapsNet (0.41), ResNet (0.35), Ensemble (0.06)

classification_or_output_head.description: Combined decision of VGG16, CapsNet, and ResNet using majority weighting scheme for binary classification (benign vs malignant).

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Proposed Ensemble (Combined Decision of VGGNet, CapsNet, and ResNet)
conclusions: The combined decision of deep learners (ensemble of VGGNet, CapsNet, and ResNet) is superior to individual learners in terms of sensitivity, accuracy, specificity, F-score, and precision for skin cancer detection.

The proposed ensemble model achieved an accuracy of 93.5%, outperforming individual models (VGGNet: 79%, CapsNet: 75%, ResNet: 69%) and other machine learning approaches (SVM, KNN, Naive Bayes, Random Forest).
The ensemble model achieved the best loss function value of 0.06, compared to 0.40, 0.41, and 0.35 for VGGNet, CapsNet, and ResNet respectively.
The study demonstrates that ensemble learning techniques can enhance prediction accuracy for sensitive medical issues like cancer detection by exploiting the diversity of learners.
The experimental results provide a compelling reason to apply this ensemble approach for other disease detection tasks.
Future work intends to study the achievement of reinforcement learning-based techniques for skin cancer detection.
important_remarks: The study uses a highly curated, balanced subset of the ISIC dataset (3000 malignant, 2800 benign) rather than the full imbalanced dataset, which may not reflect real-world clinical prevalence.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) to validate the superiority of the ensemble model over individual learners.
No external validation was performed; the model was evaluated solely on a single public dataset split, limiting generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.
Data leakage risk exists as the paper does not explicitly confirm that patient-level separation was maintained between training and testing sets, only image counts.

The ensemble approach increases computational complexity and training time (109s for the ensemble vs. 106s for VGGNet) without a proportional gain in efficiency, though accuracy improved significantly.
The study focuses only on binary classification (benign vs. malignant) and does not address multi-class skin cancer detection or other lesion types.
No data augmentation techniques were employed, relying solely on the provided dataset size, which may limit robustness against overfitting compared to studies using extensive augmentation.
